section: Chapter: When People Rebel – 1857 and After

concepts.lesson: The Revolt of 1857 was one of the first major uprisings against British rule in India. It began in May 1857 when Indian soldiers (sepoys) in the British army revolted against their officers. The revolt quickly spread to several regions of northern and central India. Many rulers, peasants and common people joined the uprising to oppose British control. Although the revolt was eventually suppressed by the British, it became an important event in the history of India's struggle against colonial rule.
concepts.real_example: The revolt started at Meerut in 1857 and soon spread to Delhi and other regions.
key_points: Spread across northern and central India

concepts.lesson: Sepoys were Indian soldiers employed in the British East India Company's army. They played an important role in maintaining British power in India. However, many sepoys were unhappy with British policies, low pay, and discrimination. Their anger increased due to the introduction of new rifle cartridges that were believed to be greased with cow and pig fat, which hurt the religious beliefs of both Hindu and Muslim soldiers.
concepts.real_example: Indian soldiers in the British army refusing to use the new Enfield rifle cartridges.

concepts.lesson: The immediate cause of the Revolt of 1857 was the introduction of the new Enfield rifle cartridges. These cartridges had to be bitten open before use and were rumored to be greased with cow and pig fat. This offended both Hindu and Muslim sepoys because cows are sacred to Hindus and pigs are considered unclean by Muslims. The anger among sepoys led to rebellion in Meerut in May 1857.
concepts.real_example: Sepoys refusing to bite the greased cartridges of the Enfield rifle.
key_points: Greased with cow and pig fat

concepts.lesson: Several leaders played important roles in the Revolt of 1857. Bahadur Shah Zafar was declared the leader of the revolt in Delhi. Rani Lakshmibai of Jhansi fought bravely against British forces. Nana Sahib led the revolt in Kanpur, and Begum Hazrat Mahal played an important role in Lucknow. These leaders helped organize resistance against British rule.
concepts.real_example: Rani Lakshmibai fighting the British army in Jhansi.
key_points: Bahadur Shah Zafar led in Delhi
Begum Hazrat Mahal led in Lucknow

concepts.lesson: After starting in Meerut, the revolt spread quickly to many parts of northern and central India. Important centers of the revolt included Delhi, Kanpur, Lucknow, Jhansi and Bareilly. Soldiers, peasants, artisans and local rulers joined the uprising in different regions. The revolt showed widespread anger against British rule.
concepts.real_example: The revolt spreading from Meerut to Delhi and other cities.
key_points: Spread to Delhi, Kanpur, Lucknow and Jhansi

concepts.lesson: The British used strong military force to suppress the Revolt of 1857. They recaptured important cities and defeated the rebel leaders. Many rebels were executed or punished severely. By 1858, the British had regained control over most of the areas affected by the revolt.
concepts.real_example: British forces capturing Delhi again and arresting Bahadur Shah Zafar.

concepts.lesson: After the Revolt of 1857, major changes were made in the administration of India. The British government ended the rule of the East India Company and took direct control of India. The Governor-General was given the new title of Viceroy. The British also reorganized the army and adopted new policies to prevent future rebellions.
concepts.real_example: The British Crown taking direct control of India in 1858.
